bins.name: Data Centers Pay Their Own Costs
bins.desc: Sierra Club and its members consistently argue that all financial costs created by large load interconnection must fall on data center owners and developers, not on residential ratepayers. The filing cites existing harm to PJM ratepayers and frames the grid as an asset built and paid for by ordinary consumers. Full costs include not only transmission upgrades but also health and community impacts.
quotes: Sierra Club agrees that changes are necessary to incorporate new large loads into the transmission system without raising costs and jeopardizing reliability for the hundreds of millions of Americans who rely on the grid.
The rapid proliferation of data centers has already caused significant harms to existing ratepayers across the country.
Ratepayers in PJM, however, have seen an outsized impact.
The costs to power data centers should be paid for by data center owners, not residential ratepayers.
Electric system design should prioritize all consumers, especially small residential consumers typically underrepresented in decision-making.

These members are frustrated that they are being forced to pay the financial and reliability costs that hyperscalar data centers and a handful of technology elitists are imposing on a system that is not currently equipped to meet this moment of unprecedented growth.

We need you to make it clear that our power system—which was paid for by everyday people across American communities paying their utility bills—serves those customers, not billionaires who own technology companies.

Electric system design should not give preference to larger industrial users, such as data centers, at the expense of residential and other consumers, including small consumers, and particularly residential ratepayers, who are typically underrepresented in decision-making processes.
The costs– financial as well as health and other community costs–to power data centers should be paid for by technology companies and developers, not residential ratepayers.

bins.name: Clean Low-Cost Energy for New Demand
bins.desc: Sierra Club urges FERC to encourage policies that bring clean, low-cost energy technologies such as solar, wind, battery storage, and geothermal online to serve rising demand from large loads, treating clean energy as both the affordability and reliability answer.
quotes: FERC should encourage policies to ensure that the lowest-cost energy technologies, including abundant, clean energy like solar, wind, battery power, and geothermal come online to match rising electric demand and ensure grid reliability.

bins.name: PJM Ratepayers Disproportionately Harmed
bins.desc: The filing specifically highlights PJM ratepayers as having faced outsized impacts from the rapid proliferation of data centers, and notes that 3,844 Sierra Club members in the PJM footprint signed letters urging action.
quotes: The rapid proliferation of data centers has already caused significant harms to existing ratepayers across the country.
Ratepayers in PJM, however, have seen an outsized impact.

bins.name: Clean Energy Meets Rising Demand
bins.desc: Sierra Club calls on FERC to steer new large load demand toward clean energy resources, framing solar, wind, battery power, and geothermal as the preferred technologies for matching rising grid demand while maintaining reliability.
quotes: FERC should encourage policies to ensure that the lowest-cost energy technologies, including abundant, clean energy like solar, wind, battery power, and geothermal come online to match rising electric demand and ensure grid reliability.

bins.name: Protect State Clean Energy Rights
bins.desc: Sierra Club and its chapter directors argue that federal large load interconnection rules must not undercut state authority to pursue climate and clean energy policies. The concern is that haste to accommodate data centers could override or weaken state-level clean energy goals.
quotes: State efforts to pursue clean energy policies, especially around climate and clean energy, should not be undermined in the rush to power data centers.

Who will pay for the costs imposed across the system by new large loads? How will the connection of these large loads to the grid affect states' abilities to meet their climate and clean energy goals?

bins.name: Grid Serves All Consumers Equally
bins.desc: The filing argues that grid design and cost allocation should prioritize all consumers, especially small residential customers who are underrepresented in regulatory proceedings, and should not give preference to large industrial users like data centers.
quotes: Electric system design should prioritize all consumers, especially small residential consumers typically underrepresented in decision-making.

We need you to make it clear that our power system—which was paid for by everyday people across American communities paying their utility bills—serves those customers, not billionaires who own technology companies.

Electric system design should not give preference to larger industrial users, such as data centers, at the expense of residential and other consumers, including small consumers, and particularly residential ratepayers, who are typically underrepresented in decision-making processes.

bins.name: Reliability Must Not Be Compromised
bins.desc: Sierra Club frames changes to large load interconnection as necessary to avoid jeopardizing reliability for hundreds of millions of existing grid users, and notes that members object to bearing reliability costs imposed by data centers on a system not equipped for this scale of growth.
quotes: Sierra Club agrees that changes are necessary to incorporate new large loads into the transmission system without raising costs and jeopardizing reliability for the hundreds of millions of Americans who rely on the grid.

These members are frustrated that they are being forced to pay the financial and reliability costs that hyperscalar data centers and a handful of technology elitists are imposing on a system that is not currently equipped to meet this moment of unprecedented growth.

bins.name: Health and Community Costs Included
bins.desc: The chapter directors' letter explicitly broadens the cost responsibility principle to include not only financial costs but also health and other community costs imposed by data centers, arguing these too must be borne by technology companies rather than local communities.
quotes: The costs– financial as well as health and other community costs–to power data centers should be paid for by technology companies and developers, not residential ratepayers.
